title: Unit 17 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Adverbs & Expressions of Manner

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 17: Adverbs and Expressions of Manner. In Unit 6, we learned that adjectives are chameleons... they never stop changing. But today... we meet the easiest words in the Greek language. Adverbs. They are completely 'frozen'. They never change their shape. I'm William... and here with me is Athina. Athina... why are adverbs so predictable?

Γεια σας! They are predictable because they don't care about the noun. They only care about the action. They describe how... where... when... or how much something happens. And you only have to learn one form for each.
One and done. Let’s look at the first group: Manner. How do we do something? Athina... tell us about the '-a' trick.

This is a great shortcut. You can turn almost any adjective into an adverb by changing its ending to 'A'. It looks exactly like the neuter plural! 'Ka-lós'... good... becomes 'Ka-lá'... well. 'Ghrée-gho-ros'... fast... becomes 'Ghrée-gho-ra'... quickly. 'O-ré-os'... nice... becomes 'O-ré-a'... nicely.
The '-a' trick is a lifesaver. Now... where is the action happening? Adverbs of Place. Athina... give us the directions.

Listen to the anchors: e-thó... here. e-keée... there. pá-no... up. ká-to... down. mé-sa... inside. é-xo... outside. And a native tip: we love to pair these with 'a-pó'. Instead of just saying 'outside'... we say 'é-xo a-pó to spée-tee'. Outside of the house.
It flows better with 'a-pó'. Now... Time. These are the anchors for our tenses. But we have a major 'Spelling Alert' here. Athina... what is the difference between 'Pó-te' and 'Po-té'?

This is very important! The stress changes the meaning. 'Pó-te'... with the stress at the start... means 'When?'. But 'Po-té'... with the stress at the end... means 'Never'. Don't mix them up... or you'll be never when you mean when!
That’s a classic mistake. And wait... when we say 'Never' in Greek... we use a Double Negative?
Yes! Like in Spanish or Italian. We say 'Den pée-no po-té'. Literally... 'I don't drink never'. It sounds perfectly correct in Greek to use two negatives. It makes it even stronger!

Double strength. The same goes for 'ka-thó-lou'... not at all. 'Den kksé-ro ka-thó-lou'... I don't know at all. Finally... Quantity. 'Po-leée' for much... 'Leée-gho' for a little... and 'ar-ke-tá' for enough.
Ακριβώς. You use these to add flavor to your actions. 'Thou-leée-vo po-leée'... I work a lot. 'Koe-má-mai leée-gho'... I sleep a little.

You have now completed the exhaustive Masterclass for Unit 17. You’ve mastered the frozen words... you know the '-a' trick... and you’ve conquered the double negatives and the 'Pó-te' versus 'Po-té' trap. Your sentences are about to get a lot more descriptive. Review the directions and commands vocabulary to see these adverbs in play. Excellent work... we’ll see you in Unit 18... where we meet the Deponent Verbs.
Συγχαρητήρια! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα.
